turns.name: KEVIN T. BAINE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the This case is before the Court for review of a final judgment of civil contempt against the two National Organizations of Catholic Bishops. It is the Bishops' contention that the contempt judgment against them is void because that judgment, and the underlying orders, were beyond the constitutional power of the District Court. The facts can be stated simply. The Respondents brought suit seeking an order directing the Commissioner of Internal Revenue to revoke the tax-exemption of the Catholic allegedly engaged in impermissible political activities in the area of abortion. The Respondents served subpoena duces tecum upon the the judicial power that is conferred by Article III, and as this Court said in United States v. Morton Salt, the subpoena power is therefore subject to the limitations of Article III. What that means --

turns.text: Mr. Baine, certainly there's language in Blair v. United States, as you know, that it certainly cuts concerns the subpoena power of the Grand Jury, and what the Court held in that case is that one who is subpoenaed to appear before a grand jury cannot object on the ground that the investigation is unconstitutional. That kind of a challenge is power of a court in a civil case, does not depend upon the existence of a case or controversy, and, in fact, in a case of the grand jury, as the Court said in Blair, the grand jury and the court don't even know whether there's a matter within the jurisdiction of the federal courts until the end of the investigation. at the beginning of the case. One can determine at the beginning of the case whether there is jurisdiction, whether that a final judgment of civil contempt against a non-party witness is immediately appealable and that the contemnor is entitled to a full review of his claims. What the Respondents say is that the Bishops could because the subpoenas were unduly burdensome, that it cost too much to xerox the documents, or that it would take too many man hours to search for the documents, but they say that we can't raise on appeal the more fundamental question whether or not the subpoenas were invalid all together because they were beyond the power of a District Court. What that overlooks is that the first issue on any appeal is the question of jurisdiction or power, not just of the Court of Appeals, but of the District Court. The appeal in this case is really no different from any other appeal in that respect. The Court of Appeals had an obligation to satisfy itself that the District Court had power

turns.name: KEVIN T. BAINE

Valley Forge was the Court said is that Article III standing -- that without Article III standing, a plaintiff may not litigate in the federal courts, that it is an essential ingredient of the Court's Article III Power, that in the absence of Article III standing, there simply is no case or controversy. are not parties. There will never be another final judgment against the Bishops that they can appeal. appeal whereas a party does not because a party will be

turns.name: KEVIN T. BAINE
turns.text: The issue in Blair was a little r> different. The problem of Blair wasn't that the witness who is not directly affected by government actions. Plaintiffs have standing to challenge executive action only when they have suffered a concrete injury thereby, and one principle that the Court has made clear time and time again is that the courts will not entertain challenges brought by citizens whose interest is no more than a generalized interest in having the Government obey or enforce the law. In the words of this Court, the federal judiciary is not an ombudsman of the general welfare. It is not designed to act as a continuing monitor of the wisdom and soundness of theoretical and abstract that acceptance of it would almost completely obliterate this principle that limits judicial even a weaker case than Chaney because it's not -- in Chaney, enforcement action. You don't even have anything like that here. All you have is the allegation that the IRS has not as yet taken any action against the Church. We don't know what they're likely to do in the future.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is there an Establishment Clause challenge being made? District Court called Establishment Clause standing, and that just falls back on the basic claim of favoritism. This- is -- what civil contempt, which is the issue here, not criminal contempt, the Court, in ruling that civil contempt orders are appealable, has limited the issues that can be raised to those issues that new class of appealable order involving jurisdictional issues. raise an issue unless it's independent of the main action?

turns.name: MARSHALL BEIL
turns.text: Well, the cases that have all come up have appeals, Alexander, Ryan, Cobbledick, all the issues were issues that relate specifically to the witness. There were questions. Was there undue burden. Was there First Amendment privilege. Was there a Fifth Amendment privilege. In personam There is no case that says a witness can raise issues that are intimately connected to the main action, such as subject matter jurisdiction, but there are cases that say witnesses can do that and that's in the Blair line of cases, and Blair says that a witness cannot raise issues going to the

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, Blair is your closest case to support that proposition?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Beil, do you think it's open -- it was open to the Court of Appeals sua sponte to determine that there was no Article III standing and to dismiss?

turns.name: MARSHALL BEIL
turns.text: I think the Court has to be very careful. I think the Court --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Was it open to the Court to do that?

turns.name: MARSHALL BEIL
turns.text: Yes. I think the Court of Appeals --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is it open to this Court to do that as well? but it's a power that should not be exercised, and the reason for it is this. At this point, there is no special rule for appeals of jurisdictional determinations. when a District Court denies a motion to dismiss for subject matter If that is challenged on a mandamus petition, the regular rules for mandamus apply. There are no special rules for mandamus for jurisdictional issues. To exercise supervisory power over the lower courts in this context would essentially throw out all of that case law, which is now 200 years old, and create a special rule for jurisdictional issues which this Court has so far declined to will wind up having repeated appeals of jurisdictional issues in any case where jurisdiction is a close case. Look at, for example, the history of this case. This case is now nearly eight years old. The District Court has made two determinations on jurisdictional issues. The Court of Appeals has been asked to review jurisdictional issues two times, and this Court has been asked to review the to raise jurisdictional issues on their appeal, then this case, which is now unusual, is going to become the norm in any case in which jurisdictional --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: why should we start off regarding the matter as somehow the primary focus should be the case out of which this order from the court issued? As far as the person who receives an order from the court that says you do this or only one case. That is the order from the Court. Does the Court have authority to issue that order? What difference does it make what litigation it comes out of it?

turns.name: MARSHALL BEIL
turns.text: But what is the injury that that party is

turns.name: MARSHALL BEIL
turns.text: Well, I think actually the focus is not on the fine because that's one that's created by his refusal to produce documents. The focus should be on the injury created by compliance, and that's not, in fact, an injury at all. That's a duty. This Court has held several times, all citizens have the obligation to come forward and produce evidence in a case if they have relevant evidence. That's a duty that I don't think would raise an injury. Certainly, if the Court is going to hold otherwise, right to appeal a jurisdictional determination that is made in an underlying case, then it would have to hold by logic that the burden placed on a defendant, which is much greater, he's going to defend the case, discovery and trial and judgment and get a judgment immediately when he's not in control of the lawsuit.

turns.name: MARSHALL BEIL
turns.text: I'm not sure I understand the question. to the party in the lawsuit, he can, at great expense, but he can get a determination. He can take a default judgment and appeal the jurisdiction of the court in the default judgment. Correct?

turns.name: MARSHALL BEIL
turns.text: Yes. saying there's no appeal, you're saying there's no right. If you get an order from the court, that's it. that as a third party witness, the issue of jurisdiction -- he takes the court as he finds it, the determination by the District Court of its jurisdiction is a determination that the District Court has the right to make and that simply is not reversible. The injury that the non-party witness has been called forth to produce that evidence suffers, which is compliance with a public duty to produce documents, doesn't give rise to an appealable injury on the jurisdictional issue because, if it does, it would mean that any denial of a motion to dismiss on jurisdictional issues would have to be automatically appealable. I don't think this Court -- this Court has certainly never held that. not only repeated appeals, because, after all, each witness that comes forward and is subpoenaed and doesn't produce documents can raise the issue again because he's not -- there's been no res judicata, you also have the possibility of circuits around the country, because a witness can only be subpoenaed in his home state, in his home district, in fact, and can raise any challenges to that subpoena in his home district. So, if you had a case, as this is like the case may elsewhere in the country, it could raise a jurisdictional issue in another District Court and a second circuit, another circuit, would be looking at it, and you would have inconsistent -- conceivably have inconsistent rulings by different courts on the same issue in the same case that could not be resolved by this Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I assume if the appeals are frivolous, appeals, and certainly a decision by one district or one So that the issue can be raised in a different circuit without being frivolous or somehow improper. I would like to turn now to the other issue in the case, which is the question of standing. Respondents do not believe response to Justice White's questions about what colorable What the Court of Appeals recognized is that there is essentially a safety valve that exists in the federal system for determinations by District Courts that are totally bereft of any rational argument.

turns.name: MARSHALL BEIL

turns.name: MARSHALL BEIL
turns.text: Well, I think -- I don't think the Court of Appeals was doing that. I think what the Court of Appeals was recognizing is that jurisdictional determinations are not appealable, but you can bring a mandamus petition to challenge challenge here was the equivalent of a mandamus petition raising a question of whether there was a usurpation of power. In fact, the District Court -- the Court of Appeals it was, in fact, applying a mandamus standard here.

turns.name: MARSHALL BEIL

It is not that the District Court is right or that the District Court is wrong, but that there is essentially a total absence of any power here. It's the standard that this case has always applied to mandamus petitions challenging jurisdictional determinations.

On the standing question, and to suggest that this case differs from Allen v. The Respondents here do not seek to be able to use tax-exempt money in political campaigns, but I don't think that they have to -- that that makes a difference because when the question is unequal treatment, the Court has two alternatives to remedy that. One is to extend the remedy to the disfavored group or the other is to withdraw it from the favored group, and this Court has held in various cases that deal with how to remedy under-inclusive statutes and unequal treatment cases that the the Chaplain Corps in the services, saying that they aren't chaplains for some religious groups, although there should be. be done either way, either by extending or by removing, and You cannot expect the political process to correct 24 between permissible and impermissible political activities in You don't mandats that the Attorney General go off and prosecute everybody else.
